topic: pothole season alignment work flooding in

turns.content: man I am slammed right now. you got any tips for managing a backlog when youre a small shop

Absolutely. When you're a small operation, a backlog can either be a goldmine or a customer service nightmare depending on how you handle it. A few things that work well:

1. **Triage by urgency** — safety stuff first (brakes, steering), then scheduled work, then cosmetic or non-urgent jobs.
2. **Be honest about wait times** — tell people upfront it's a 3-day wait or whatever it is. Most customers respect honesty more than a promise you can't keep.
3. **Batch similar jobs** — if you've got 5 alignments waiting, knock them all out in a row. Your guys get into a rhythm and move faster.

What's driving the rush? Seasonal spike, or did something specific happen?
potholes man. the roads around here got torn up bad from that ice we had back in the winter and the city still hasnt fixed half of them. everybody and their mother needs an alignment now

That tracks. Winter ice damage creates potholes, spring rain makes them worse, and by summer people have been driving on misaligned vehicles for months. The ones coming in now are probably the folks who kept putting it off until their tires started wearing funny or the steering pull got too bad to ignore.

The good news is alignment work is solid margin work and it's quick once you're set up. Are you able to turn these around same-day, or is the wait pushing into next-day territory?
we're booking 2 to 3 days out right now. I got guys working steady and we still cant keep up. good problem to have I guess

Definitely a good problem. Two to three days out is manageable and shows demand is strong. A few ways to maximize this while it lasts:

- **Upsell the inspection** — when a car is on the rack for alignment, check the tie rods, ball joints, and control arm bushings. Pothole damage doesn't stop at alignment. That's added revenue per ticket.
- **Book the next appointment** — tell every alignment customer "these roads are still a mess, come back in 6 months for a recheck." Plants the seed for return business.
- **Prioritize repeat customers** — if someone's been coming to you for years, squeeze them in sooner. Loyalty pays off long term.

Are you seeing any suspension damage too, or is it mostly just alignment pulls?
oh yeah Im seeing bent control arms, blown struts, the works. one lady came in yesterday with a cracked rim from a pothole on Poplar Ave. said she hit it going about 40

Poplar Ave potholes are no joke. A cracked rim at 40 mph means that was a serious hole — she's lucky it wasn't worse than a rim. That's a good reminder to check for tire sidewall damage on those too. A bulge or bubble on the inside sidewall can blow out later and people don't think to look.

The bent control arms and blown struts are where the real money is on these jobs. An alignment is $80-100, but when it turns into a control arm replacement and alignment, you're talking $400-600 per vehicle. Make sure your guys are documenting every bit of damage with photos — helps the customer understand the repair and protects you if there's ever a question.
yeah we been taking pictures of everything. I learned that the hard way a couple years back. customer tried to say we broke something that was already broke

Smart. Before-photos are your best friend in this business. One clear picture of a worn-out part before you touch anything saves you a world of headache. Shops that don't document are playing with fire.

Sounds like you've got a good system going. The wave won't last forever, but while these potholes are still eating cars alive, make the most of it. Stack that revenue.
thats the plan brother. just gotta keep my guys moving and not let anybody burn out. 10 hour days all week

Keep an eye on that. Burned-out techs start making mistakes, and mistakes cost more than the extra hours earn. If you can, throw them a bonus or buy lunch a couple days a week. Small stuff like that goes a long way when guys are grinding hard.

Maybe even give them a half day on a Saturday if you can swing it. A little goodwill now keeps your best people from looking elsewhere when things slow down.
yeah I been buying lunch every day this week. least I can do. alright man gotta get back out there. appreciate the advice
That's good leadership right there. Go make that money while the work is flowing in. Good luck with the backlog.
